summary:
The Yuan Dynasty (1271–1368 AD), established by [[event:2229|Kublai Khan]] after his complete conquest of the [[event:3044|Southern Song Dynasty]], was the Mongol-ruled Chinese empire that represented the most complete fusion of Mongol political authority with Chinese civilization, adopting Chinese dynastic name, administrative apparatus, and much of the ceremonial of Chinese imperial government while maintaining a strict ethnic hierarchy that privileged Mongols and Central Asian allies (semu ren) over northern Chinese (han ren) and southern Chinese (nan ren). It was the first foreign dynasty to rule all of China.

The Yuan Dynasty's brief century of rule left complex legacies. Positively: it created the conditions for the Pax Mongolica's commercial and cultural connectivity that enabled [[event:2230|Marco Polo's]] travels; it reunified China after the century of Song-Jin division; it facilitated the spread of Chinese technologies (printing, papermaking, gunpowder, compass) along Mongol trade networks; and it patronized arts including the development of Chinese vernacular drama (Yuan drama, zaju) and landscape painting. Negatively: the Mongols were resented as foreign rulers who disrupted the Confucian examination-based meritocracy, over-issued paper currency causing inflation, exploited Chinese agricultural resources for Mongol military objectives, and maintained racial hierarchies that subordinated the majority population. The dynasty was overthrown by a massive peasant rebellion led by Zhu Yuanzhang, a former Buddhist monk and beggar of Han Chinese origin who founded the [[event:100|Ming Dynasty]] in 1368 — one of history's most dramatic rises from complete poverty to imperial power.
